instructions:
You are an expert software engineer debugging C/C++ programs. You
interact with a Unix shell to investigate bugs and produce diagnoses.

queries.user_text:
You're debugging a real-codebase bug in `cjson-parse-string-oob` (an open-source C/C++ project).

You are at the project root. The buggy binary is at `./bench_driver`. The bug was injected by a small patch — somewhere in the source tree, a guard / check / initializer was removed or weakened.

Use bash to navigate the source tree, reproduce the failure, localize the defect, and propose both a local fix and a structural global fix.

Your final response MUST include three labelled paragraphs:

  ROOT CAUSE: <file:line and what is wrong, in your own words>
  LOCAL FIX:  <minimal code change that resolves the symptom>
  GLOBAL FIX: <structural change preventing this CLASS of bug>

Do NOT modify the source files on disk. Just investigate and produce the diagnosis as your final assistant message.
queries.prompt:
You're debugging a real-codebase bug in `cjson-parse-string-oob` (an open-source C/C++ project).

You are at the project root. The buggy binary is at `./bench_driver`. The bug was injected by a small patch — somewhere in the source tree, a guard / check / initializer was removed or weakened.

Use bash to navigate the source tree, reproduce the failure, localize the defect, and propose both a local fix and a structural global fix.

Your final response MUST include three labelled paragraphs:

  ROOT CAUSE: <file:line and what is wrong, in your own words>
  LOCAL FIX:  <minimal code change that resolves the symptom>
  GLOBAL FIX: <structural change preventing this CLASS of bug>

Do NOT modify the source files on disk. Just investigate and produce the diagnosis as your final assistant message.
queries.response:
THOUGHT: I’ll start by listing the repository contents and running the provided `bench_driver` to reproduce the failure and see any sanitizer/crash output.

---

THOUGHT: The binary exits quietly with no arguments, so I need to inspect the harness to understand how it feeds cJSON and what input triggers the “parse string OOB” failure. I’ll read the driver and relevant parse-string code, and also look for any available sanitizer binary behavior.

---

THOUGHT: The driver comment already points at malformed string input. Running without stdin yields no crash, so I’ll feed an unterminated JSON string and inspect the parser implementation around `parse_string`, especially its bounds predicates.

---

THOUGHT: The ASan run with an unterminated JSON string reproduces the heap read past the allocated buffer in `parse_string`. The working tree diff already points to a one-line injected change: the scan for the closing quote no longer checks `input_buffer->length` before dereferencing. I’ll collect exact line numbers and surrounding parser setup to make the diagnosis precise, including how `length` is defined and why the NUL terminator does not make the current loop safe.

---

THOUGHT: I reproduced the crash with an unterminated JSON string (`"unterminated`) under ASan. The failing read is in `parse_string` while it is searching for the closing quote. `git diff` shows the injected patch removed the bounds check from that scan loop, changing `while (((size_t)(input_end - input_buffer->content) < input_buffer->length) && (*input_end != '\"'))` to `while (*input_end != '\"')`. Because `cJSON_ParseWithOpts` computes `buffer.length` as `strlen(value) + 1`, the parse buffer length includes the terminating NUL but not bytes beyond the allocated buffer. With no closing quote, the weakened loop first reads the NUL inside the buffer, then increments `input_end` past the allocation and dereferences it again at the loop condition, causing the heap-buffer-overflow. The later check at lines 813-816 is too late because the OOB read already occurred in the loop condition.

ROOT CAUSE: `cJSON.c:798` in `parse_string` has an unbounded loop condition, `while (*input_end != '\"')`, when scanning for the terminating quote of a JSON string. The removed guard allowed `input_end` to advance beyond `input_buffer->length`; for an unterminated string, the loop dereferences one byte past the heap buffer before the subsequent “string ended unexpectedly” check can run.

LOCAL FIX: Restore the input-buffer bounds check in the scan loop so the code never dereferences `input_end` unless it is still within `parse_buffer.length`:
```diff
diff --git a/cJSON.c b/cJSON.c
@@
-        while (*input_end != '\"')
+        while (((size_t)(input_end - input_buffer->content) < input_buffer->length) && (*input_end != '\"'))
         {
             /* is escape sequence */
             if (input_end[0] == '\\')
```
The existing post-loop check then correctly reports `goto fail` when no quote is found before the end of the parse buffer.

GLOBAL FIX: Stop doing raw pointer sentinel scans over parser input. Represent the remaining input as a bounded slice/cursor (`ptr`, `end` or `base`, `offset`, `length`) and centralize operations such as `peek`, `advance`, and `find_unescaped_quote` so every read is guarded by `cursor < end`. Add sanitizer-backed regression/fuzz tests for unterminated strings and trailing backslash cases through both `cJSON_ParseWithOpts` and `cJSON_ParseWithLengthOpts`, making out-of-bounds reads in token scanners a CI failure.

tool_calls.call: ls -la && find . -maxdepth 2 -type f | sed 's#^./##' | sort | head -200
git status --short && git diff -- cJSON.c | sed -n '1,200p'
